concerns.text: The plan defines 'Regioisomer Diversity Count' by counting distinct product SMILES in the dataset. However, USPTO-50k typically lists a single canonical product SMILES per reaction entry, even if multiple isomers are theoretically possible. The plan assumes the dataset 'explicitly lists multiple products' or that 'distinct canonical SMILES' can be derived from a single string. If the dataset only contains one product per reaction, the target variable will be a constant (1) or zero, rendering regression impossible. The methodology must define how to derive the *count* of possible regioisomers (e.g., via reaction template enumeration) rather than counting existing entries.

The plan assumes the USPTO-50k dataset contains 'Reaction Type' metadata sufficient to filter for EAS. Standard USPTO-50k splits often lack explicit reaction class labels, requiring inference via SMARTS pattern matching on reactants/products. The plan mentions 'pattern matching' but does not specify the SMARTS patterns or the validation method for the EAS filter. Without a verified, high-precision filter, the dataset may be contaminated with non-EAS reactions, invalidating the specific hypothesis about EAS selectivity.

The plan proposes Poisson Regression for 'Regioisomer Diversity Count'. If the target variable is derived as a count of distinct products and most reactions yield exactly one product (count=1), the data will be heavily zero-inflated or one-inflated. Standard Poisson regression assumes the mean equals the variance; if the variance is near zero (constant target), the model will fail to converge or produce meaningless p-values. The methodology needs a contingency plan (e.g., Zero-Inflated Poisson or treating the target as a binary 'diversity vs. no diversity' classification) if the distribution is degenerate.
